segments.s120-g17: بکند is a third-person narrative verb form (classical usage) introducing the action 'he pulled/removed (it)'; و is the coordinating 'and' joining two actions; چو here is the temporal 'when' introducing the subordinate clause; ژوپین is a garment (a padded jacket) and appears as the direct object of برگرفت; به + کف 'into/into the palm' shows direction/place; برگرفت is the past (perfective) verb 'took up, raised' that completes the temporal clause 'when he took the gambeson into his palm.'
segments.s120-g18: بماندند is the past plural 'they remained'; لشکر همه is the subject phrase 'the whole army' (لشکر 'army' modified/quantified by همه 'all/everyone'); در شگفت is a prepositional phrase 'in wonder/astonishment' (شگفت here used as a noun, 'wonder').
segments.s120-g19: بینداخت is a past/preterite verb 'he threw/cast (it down)'; چون functions here as the temporal 'when'; نزد ایشان رسید is a short clause 'he reached/arrived near them' (نزد 'near/in the presence of', ایشان 'them', رسید past 'arrived').
segments.s120-g20: سواران is the plural subject 'horsemen/riders'; بسی is an adverbial intensifier here (classical usage often meaning 'many' or 'much'); زیر شاخ is a prepositional phrase 'under/beneath the bough(s)' (زیر + شاخ); آورید is a past/plural narrative form 'they brought / they moved' completing the clause 'the horsemen brought themselves/rode beneath the boughs.'
segments.s120-g21: یکی is an indefinite pronoun 'one / someone / a certain (person)'; دست بگرفت is a verb phrase 'seized the hand' (بگرفت past 'took/seized'); و links the two coordinated actions; بفشاردَش is a causative/active verb with enclitic object '-ش' 'he pressed/squeezed him/it' (here: pressed the hand or pressed him).
segments.s120-g22: همی is a classical adverb marking repeated/continuous action ('kept on / continually'); آزمون را uses را as the definite object marker with آزمون 'the test/trial' as object; بیازاردش is a verb with enclitic object '-ش' meaning 'he/they tortured/vexed him' or 'caused him distress'—together 'they kept tormenting him with that trial.'
segments.s120-g23: بخندید is a past tense verb 'laughed'; ازو here marks the target/source 'at him / from him' (classical use with خندیدن often means 'laughed at'); رستم پیلتن places the proper name 'Rostam' with a geographic/epithet 'of Piltan' following it (a name plus epithet).
segments.s120-g24: شده is the past participle/auxiliary 'became' or 'was made'; خیره is an adjective/participial state 'staring / astonished' that complements شده; ز (short for از) means 'from / because of' (here 'because of him'); چشم آن انجمن is a genitive phrase 'the eyes of that assembly' (چشم 'eye', آن 'that/the', انجمن 'assembly'), so the clause reads 'the assembly's eyes became fixed/astonished because of him.'
segments.s120-g1: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. نامه is the nominative/accusative noun 'the letter'. به مهر is a prepositional phrase 'with a seal' (مهر = seal) qualifying the letter. اندر آورد is a past tense verb phrase 'brought in / presented'; شاه at the end is the indirect object 'to the king' (Persian often places the recipient after the verb).
segments.s120-g2: جهانجوی is an epithet 'world‑seeking' modifying the following name. رستم is the proper name. بپیموده is a perfect participle/past form 'having traversed / had traversed' (the b- prefix is archaic/poetic marking completed action). راه is the object 'the road/path'.
segments.s120-g3: به زین is a locative phrase 'onto/at the saddle' (به + noun). اندر افگند is the past tense verb 'threw/cast into/on' (اندر here indicates 'into/in' as part of the verbal phrase). گرز گران places the noun 'mace' before its postpositive adjective 'heavy' (گران = heavy/weighty).
segments.s120-g4: چو is again temporal 'when'. آمد is simple past 'he came/approached'. به نزدیک is a prepositional phrase 'to/near' functioning adverbially ('approached/near'). مازندران is the place name (Mazandaran).
segments.s120-g5: به شاه آگهی شد is an impersonal/passive construction 'the king was made aware / the king received news' (آگهی شد = 'was informed'). که introduces the subordinate clause 'that'. کاووس is the subject of that clause. کی here functions as a temporal 'when' linking to the next verbal phrase.
segments.s120-g6: فرستادن نامه is a verbal noun phrase 'the sending of the letter / having sent the letter'. افگند is past 'threw/sent' (here 'sent out, dispatched'), and پی is an adverb/preposition 'after / in pursuit' modifying افگند to mean 'sent in pursuit' or 'sent after (someone)'.
segments.s120-g7: فرستاده‌ای is an indefinite noun 'a messenger' (the -ای ending gives indefiniteness). چون is a simile particle 'like/as'. هژبر is a proper name (Hejbar). دژم is an adjective 'grim/sullen' describing the messenger; adjectives may follow the noun in this style.
segments.s120-g8: کمندی is the noun 'a lasso/snare' (the thing possessed or carried). به فتراک is a locative phrase 'on/over the cloak or saddle‑cloth' (فتراک = saddlecloth/cloak). بر شست خم literally 'on the bent thumb' (بر = on; شست = thumb; خم = bent/curve), describing how the lasso is looped over the thumb.
segments.s120-g65: نگه کرد is a two-word verbal phrase: نگه (a noun 'gaze') + کرد (past of کردن used as a light verb) = 'he looked.' The follow-on بنشاند is the past (archaic/poetic) form of نشاندن 'to seat/set down' and takes its object implicitly; اندر خورش is a prepositional phrase 'in/at the meal' (اندر = 'in/at', خورش = 'a cooked dish/meal'). The conjunction و links the two past actions ('looked and seated').
segments.s120-g66: ز + [proper name] + پرسید = 'asked of/from Kavus' where ز is the poetic preposition 'of/from' that here marks the addressee/target of the question. The second و introduces a new object/topic: از لشکرش — از is 'about/concerning' and لشکرش 'his army' (لشکر + enclitic possessive -ش). The verb پرسید applies to the whole clause: 'he asked Kavus about his army.'
segments.s120-g67: سخن راند is a poetic expression meaning 'he spoke/uttered words' (سخن = 'speech; words', راند used metaphorically as 'drove/uttered'). از introduces the topics spoken of: راه و رنج دراز = 'the road and the long hardship(s)' (دراز modifies رنج: 'long').
segments.s120-g68: که here introduces a subordinate clause ('that/namely' or 'how'), and چون راندی is a question/relative phrase 'how you rode/led' with -ی marking the 2nd-person past form (poetic). اندر نشیب و فراز is a locative phrase 'in the descent and ascent' (literally 'in low and high places' = 'through valleys and heights'). Together: '…how you rode in descent and ascent.'
segments.s120-g69: ازان پس = 'after that' (ازان = 'from that/thereupon', پس = 'then/after'). بدو گفت رستم is an inverted word order common in poetry: بدو 'to him', گفت (the speech verb) and the speaker name رستم follows as subject; the whole means 'Rostam said to him.' The final توی functions as vocative ('you' in older/poetic usage) introducing direct address.
segments.s120-g70: که introduces the content of Rostam's speech ('that / namely'). داری is present/factual 'you have/possess' (دارد → داری in address). بر و بازوی پهلوی lists the things possessed: بازو = 'arm', پهلوی = 'side/flank'; و connects them. بر here functions as part of a compound/poetic phrase (rendered 'strength/power in the...' in natural English).
segments.s120-g71: چنین داد پاسخ is a formulaic clause 'he gave such an answer' (چنین = 'thus', داد = past 'gave', پاسخ = 'answer'). The following que/clause beginning with که contains the quoted reply: من چاکرم = 'I am a chākar/servant' where چاکرم is چاکر + first-person ending -م (poetic: 'I am a servant').
segments.s120-g72: This is a conditional clause introduced by اگر 'if.' چاکری را (چاکری + object marker را) names the service/subservience as the object. خود 'myself' intensifies the subject (he stresses his own person). اندر خورم uses اندر + خورم (خورم is present/subjunctive-like first-person 'I take/receive' in poetic usage) to mean 'I take upon myself' or 'I enter into.' The whole clause means 'if I, myself, take on servitude.'
segments.s120-g49: بیفشارد is an archaic/perfective verb form (be- + فشرد) meaning 'he squeezed/pressed'; in this poetic line the verb appears before its object. چنگ کلاهور is the object ('the claw of Kalahur'), with کلاهور a proper name; سخت here functions adverbially ('hard/strongly') modifying the action.
segments.s120-g50: فرو ریخت is a compound verbal phrase (فرو 'down' + ریختن 'to fall/flow') meaning 'fell down' (poetic word order places the verb before the noun). ناخن is the affected part ('nail'), and چو ... از درخت is a simile 'like a leaf from a tree' specifying how it fell.
segments.s120-g51: کلاهور is the subject (proper name). با + دست آویخته is a prepositional phrase describing his state ('with his hand hanging' / 'his hand dangling'), where آویخته is a participial adjective 'hung, dangling'.
segments.s120-g52: پی و پوست و ناخن is a coordinated list of body-parts (here: flesh/tendons, skin, and nails). فروریخته is a past-participial/adjectival verbal form meaning 'has fallen apart/collapsed/been shattered' and predicates the state of those parts (poetic verbless/state description).
segments.s120-g53: بیاورد and بنمود are past verbal actions linked by و ('he brought and showed'); با شاه گفت means 'he spoke/said to the king' (با = 'with/to', گفت here functions as the past-tense verb 'said' though the provided sense labels are nominal).
segments.s120-g54: که introduces reported content ('that ...'). بر خویشتن = 'upon oneself / on his own person' (خویشتن reflexive). درد نتوان نهفت is a modal construction: نتوان (cannot) + نهفت (infinitival/substantival form of 'to hide/keep hidden') = 'one cannot hide pain'.
segments.s120-g55: ترا (ت + را = 'to/for you') marks the beneficiary/target. آشتی بهتر آید ز جنگ is a comparative clause: 'reconciliation comes/is better for you than war' (ز functions as 'than' in poetic usage).
segments.s120-g56: فراخی مکن is an imperative negative ('do not [make/keep] generosity/openness' — here idiomatically 'do not be close-minded or stingy'). بر دل خویش تنگ targets the action: بر دل 'upon the heart', خویش (خوی + ش) = 'your own', and تنگ ('narrow') is predicative describing the heart. Together: 'Do not make your heart narrow.'
segments.s120-g57: ترا is the object-form of 'you' (ت + را) — the pronoun is split into the 2nd-person suffix (ت) and the object marker (را) as separate items here. با is the preposition 'with'; چنین modifies the following noun and means 'such'. پهلوان is the noun 'champion/hero'. تاو نیست is the predicate; تاو is a poetic/archaic noun here and the copula نیست follows it ('is not'). The whole segment means literally 'for you with such a champion there is no تاو'.
segments.s120-g58: اگر introduces a conditional clause 'if'. رام is an adjective 'tame/ subdued' and گردد (from گشت/گرد) is a present/subjunctive-style verb meaning 'becomes' or 'is made'. به از ... نیست is a comparative idiom meaning 'there is none better than ...' (literally 'better than X is not'). ساو is treated as a proper name here; نیست is the final copula 'is not'.
segments.s120-g59: پذیریم is first-person-plural hortative/subjunctive ('let us accept' / 'we will accept'). از introduces the source 'from', followed by شهر مازندران 'the city of Mazandaran' as the noun phrase that is being accepted.
segments.s120-g60: ببخشیم here is first-person-plural hortative/subjunctive of بخشیدن; context makes it 'let us give/bestow' (rather than 'forgive'). بر is used in the sense 'to/onto' (direction or recipient). کهتر و مهتران are two contrasted groups — 'کهتر' = the lesser/younger and 'مهتران' = the greater/elders/chiefs (plural). The verb applies to both coordinated nouns.
segments.s120-g61: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so' modifying the whole clause. رنج دشوار is a noun phrase 'hard/difficult suffering' with the adjective دشوار following the noun (typical Persian order: noun + adjective). آسان کنیم uses کنیم (1st-person-plural 'let us make' / 'we will make') as the light verb; together it means 'we will make ... easy'.
segments.s120-g62: به آید که is a common classical sequence meaning 'it may come to pass that / so that'. آید here has the sense 'happen/come to pass' rather than literal 'come'. جان را is the object with را marking definiteness/emphasis ('the life/soul'); هراسان کنیم is a causative construction using کنیم 'we make/cause to be frightened' — 'we cause the soul to be frightened' (i.e. we terrify him).
segments.s120-g63: تهمتن is the proper name (the hero's epithet) and is the sentence subject. بیامد is the past simple 'came'. هم is 'also' or 'too'. اندر زمان is a temporal prepositional phrase 'in/at the time' — together: 'Tahmatan also came at that time.'
segments.s120-g64: This clause centers on رساندن 'to bring/deliver'. بر شاه is the beneficiary/goal 'to the king' (بر = 'to/toward' here). برسان is the verb form 'bring/deliver' (imperative or narrative-present in older poetic usage). شیر ژیان is a compound 'lion (شیر) of life (ژیان)' used as an epithet for a brave/lively lion or a heroic figure; grammatically it is the direct object of برسان.
segments.s120-g33: بسان is a simile marker 'like/as'. پلنگ ژیان is a noun phrase where ژیان ('life'/'living') qualifies پلنگ, 'a living/leaping leopard'. بد به خوی is an adjectival phrase meaning 'bad in disposition' (بد 'bad/ill' + به خوی 'in disposition/habit'), describing the leopard’s fierce nature.
segments.s120-g34: نکردی is the past negative 'did not do' (here used narratively; classical endings can sometimes be generic). به جز is a fixed phrase 'except'. چیز آرزوی is inverted: چیز 'thing' modified by آرزوی ('of desire/wish'), i.e. 'any desired thing'; together: 'he did not do (have) anything except war' → 'he desired nothing but war.'
segments.s120-g35: پذیره شدن is a nominalized/passive construction 'the reception being made' (پذیره 'reception' + شدن auxiliary). را is the definite-object marker marking that noun phrase. فرا پیش here is an adverbial compound meaning 'forth/in abundance/ready before them', and خواند is the past 'he called/summoned'.
segments.s120-g36: به introduces the indirect object 'to/for'. مردیش is مرد + possessive -یش 'his man/hero', here 'the brave man' (heroic sense). بر چرخ گردان is a prepositional phrase 'upon the revolving wheel' (چرخ 'wheel' + گردان 'revolving'), and نشاند is an archaic past 'placed/caused to sit'.
segments.s120-g37: بدو is a clitic phrase 'to him' (i.e. 'he said to him'). گفت is the report verb 'said' (here compressed in poetry). پیش فرستاده شو is an instruction: پیش 'forward/ahead', فرستاده as past-participle/noun 'sent/messenger', and شو is the imperative of شدن 'become / be sent' → 'be sent forward (as a messenger)'.
segments.s120-g38: هنرها is the plural 'the skills/arts'. پدیدار کن is an imperative compound: پدیدار 'manifest/appear' + کن 'make/do' → 'make (them) appear'. نو به نو is an adverbial repetition 'new to new' = 'anew / afresh / repeatedly', where به links the two نو elements.
segments.s120-g39: چنان کن is an imperative 'do thus / do so'. که introduces a purpose/result clause 'so that'. گردد is the classical/subjunctive/present-passive form 'becomes' (may become). رخش is the steed's name (or 'his steed'), پر ز شرم = 'full of shame' (پر 'full (of)' + ز 'from/because of' + شرم 'shame').
segments.s120-g40: به چشم اندر آرد literally 'brings into the eye': به 'to/into', چشم 'eye', اندر 'in/into', آرد 'brings' (third-person present/past in narrative). ز شرم آب گرم is a causal phrase 'from shame (z شرم) warm water (آب گرم)' — a poetic image for hot tears brought by shame.
segments.s120-g41: بیامد is simple past 3rd‑person singular 'came'. The sequence places the verb before its subject (poetic word order). کلاهور is the proper name/subject. چون + نره شیر is a simile 'like a male lion' (چون precedes the phrase it compares).
segments.s120-g42: به پیش is a prepositional phrase 'in the presence of / before'; جهاندار is the ruler noun and مرد دلیر is an appositive epithet describing him ('the brave man'). Word order has the preposition + noun followed by its descriptive apposition.
segments.s120-g43: بپرسید is past 'he asked' (3rd‑person). پرسیدنی here is adjectival/adverbial 'in a questioning/interrogative manner' (poetic repetition to flavor the action). چون پلنگ is another simile 'like a leopard' modifying how he questioned.
segments.s120-g44: دژم روی is a compound descriptor 'grim‑faced' (دژم = grim/sullen + روی = face). زانپس is a temporal adverb 'thereupon/after that'. بدو is the indirect object 'to him'. داد is past 'gave/struck' and چنگ is the direct object 'claw/talon' — together 'thereupon he gave/struck him with a claw.'
segments.s120-g45: بیفشارد is a past verb 'pressed/squeezed' (poetic older form). It takes چنگ 'claw' as its object. سرافراز is an epithet 'proud / triumphant' modifying پیل 'elephant' (noun follows its adjective-like epithet in this order).
segments.s120-g46: شد is 'became' (past). از introduces the cause/reason 'from / because of' (here 'from pain'). دستش is 'his hand' (دست + possessive ـش). به کردار is a poetic expression 'in the manner/appearance of' and here is followed by نیل 'indigo' (color); together the clause says 'from the pain his hand became like indigo.' Note: کردار is used poetically here to indicate resemblance.
segments.s120-g47: بپیچید is past 'he turned' (or 'he turned aside'). The coordinating و links the next clause. اندیشه 'thought' is the thing kept away; ز (poetic) marks source/target 'from him'. دورداشت is past 'kept away' — together: 'he turned and kept his thought away from him.' The subject is supplied from the previous verbs.
segments.s120-g48: به here introduces possession/association ('he had [with him] / he possessed'). مردی is 'a man' and ز خورشید 'from the sun' is a poetic genitive indicating origin or sunlike provenance. منشور داشت is 'had an inscribed tablet/charter' (منشور = inscribed tablet/charter, داشت = had). The whole phrase means 'he had a man who bore an inscribed/sun‑made tablet' — a poetic way to describe a distinguished attendant.
segments.s120-g89: براندیش is an imperative (2nd-person singular) meaning “consider/reflect”; it stands before the coordinated phrase. The conjunction و simply links the two halves. تخت بزرگان is a genitive noun phrase (takhte-ye bozurgān, “the throne of the great/ great ones”) serving as the object being addressed. مجوی is a negative imperative (“do not seek”) that applies to that noun phrase; the verb comes after the object here because of the poetic word order.
segments.s120-g90: کزین is a compound prepositional phrase (from + this) meaning “from this/therefrom.” برتری خواری is a noun phrase (“preeminence/superiority” + “humiliation”) describing the consequence. آید is 3rd-person singular present (here with future implication) “comes/happens.” بروی in this poetic line functions as a fused locative/benefactive (“upon you” / “befalls you”) — an archaic/poetic locution (literally ‘بر روی’) indicating the result affects the addressee.
segments.s120-g91: سوی introduces direction (“toward”). گاه here denotes an occasion/place related to Iran (literary use of گاه as ‘place/seat’ or ‘time/occasion’), so سوی گاه ایران = “toward the (seat/time) of Iran.” بگردان is an imperative 2nd-person singular “turn!” (with the prefix ب- marking the present/imperative form). عنان (“reins”) is the direct object — literally “turn the reins (i.e. change course).”
segments.s120-g92: وگرنه is the conditional adverb “otherwise.” زمانت is a possessive noun (“your time”/your appointed moment). سرآرد is 3rd-person singular present with an intransitive/causative sense “will bring about / befall.” سنان is here the concrete noun “spear” (object/agent of death) — the clause warns that, otherwise, your time will bring the spear upon you (i.e. you will be struck/killed).
segments.s120-g93: اگر introduces a conditional protasis (“if”). با سپه من is an instrumental/associative phrase “with my army” (the doer or force). بجنبم is 1st-person singular present/subjunctive (ب- prefix + جنب + -م ending) used for a contingent/future action “I stir/move” or “if I set in motion.” ز جای is an ablative/adverbial phrase “from its place/position.”
segments.s120-g94: تو is the 2nd-person pronoun “you.” پیدا here is used in the sense “find/see” (i.e. ‘be present’). نبینی is 2nd-person singular present negative “you will not see/find.” سرت را is the definite object with the accusative marker را (“your head”), and ز پای is an ablative phrase “from the foot(s).” The whole idiomatic reading is that you will not find your head on/with your body — an idiom for being beheaded (the poetic order places the ablative after the object).
segments.s120-g95: تو is the 2nd-person pronoun “you.” افتاده‌ای is a stative/perfect form (past participle + 2nd-person ending) meaning “you have fallen” or “you are fallen.” بی‌گمان is an adverb “undoubtedly/without doubt.” در گمان is a prepositional phrase “in (the) suspicion/doubt/conjecture,” i.e. “in confusion/doubt.” Together they state the condition of the addressee.
segments.s120-g96: یکی راه برگیر is an imperative phrase: یکی (‘one/a single’) + راه (‘path/course’) as the object, with برگیر the 2nd-person singular imperative “take/choose.” The conjunction و links the next command. بفگن is an archaic/poetic imperative form (spelling/reading varies in manuscripts) acting as a verb meaning roughly “bend/draw/ready” here with کمان (“bow”) as its object; the phrase therefore commands to ready the bow. Poetic diction allows slightly altered verbs; the imperative sequence gives direct orders.
segments.s120-g113: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' introducing the source (ز مازندران = 'from Mazandaran'). هرچ ('whatever') is a free-relative determiner, followed by the two verbs دید و شنید ('he saw and he heard') in simple past; the two verbs are coordinated by و and come at the end of the clause as is normal in older Persian.
segments.s120-g114: همه ('all/everything') is the direct object, کرد is the past tense verb 'made/did' (here causative: 'made [it] ...'), while بر + شاه ایران marks the indirect object/goal ('to the Shah of Iran'); پدید is a resultative/complement meaning 'manifest/visible' (’made manifest'). The sequence 'همه کرد ... پدید' therefore means 'he made everything appear to the king.'
segments.s120-g115: وزان پس is a temporal phrase ('and/from that time, then/after that'); ورا is the enclitic accusative 'to him', and گفت is the verb 'said' (past). مندیش is a prohibitive/imperative-type form telling the addressee not to worry (classical negative imperative built on the root 'اندیش' = 'think'), and هیچ reinforces the negation ('nothing/at all').
segments.s120-g116: دلیری کن is an imperative: دلیری ('bravery') + کن (imperative 'do/make' used to mean 'be brave' or 'show bravery'). و links the next command; رزم دیوان is 'battle with the divs' (رزم = battle, دیوان = the divs/demons), and بسیچ here modifies the action as a muster/large force ('in great number'/'gather many'). The overall tone is a pair of imperatives: be brave and assemble for war against the divs.
segments.s120-g117: This noun phrase lists two coordinated groups: دلیران ('the brave ones') و گردان ('and the troop/ranks') followed by the genitive/identifying modifier آن انجمن ('of that assembly'). Word order is typical Persian: modifiers (آن انجمن) follow the head noun (گردان) and coordinate with the preceding noun دلیران.
segments.s120-g118: چنان دان is an imperative construction: چنان ('so/thus') + دان (from دانستن here used as 'consider/hold'), meaning 'consider them so.' که introduces the content clause ('that...') in which خوارند is a present-tense predication 'they are despised/lowly.' بر + چشم من is an idiom 'in my eye/sight' = 'in my estimation.' Together: 'Consider them such that they are contemptible in my eyes.'
segments.s120-g73: کجا ان-place word introduces the reference point ('where' / 'since he is there'); او + بود is the copula phrase ('he is/was') placed before the speaker clause by poetic inversion. من نیایم is a negative present/subjunctive-like form meaning 'I should not / I would not come' (نیایم = 'do not come' used here as 'I will not intervene'). به کار is the prepositional phrase 'to the affair/into the matter' and completes the clause: literally 'where he was, I would not come to the affair.'
segments.s120-g74: که here gives the reason ('for / because'). او پهلوانست is a fused epic form (پهلوان + است → پهلوانست) meaning 'he is a hero/champion.' و links coordinated items; گرد و سوار is a terse, poetic coordination meaning a host/force and horsemen — گرد in this classical sense denotes a band or host (an archaic/poetic usage not listed among the simple glosses), and سوار = 'rider/horseman'.
segments.s120-g75: بدو is the older prepositional pronoun 'to him' (به او); داد is the past 'gave'; پس here means 'then' (sequential). نامور qualifies نامه ('the famed/renowned letter'). را is the definite object marker attached to نامه, marking the whole noun phrase as the direct object.
segments.s120-g76: This is an appositive noun phrase modifying نامه: پیام = 'message'. جهانجوی and خودکامه are postpositive modifiers (typical Persian order: noun then modifiers), here describing the intended addressee: جهانجوی = 'world-seeking' / 'ambitious (person)', خودکامه = 'self-willed / autocratic'. The final را again marks the whole phrase as an object/topic in the larger clause.
segments.s120-g77: بگفت is the narrative past 'he said / he told'. آنک is a conjunction/relativizer 'that' introducing reported content. شمشیر بار آورد is a verbal phrase: شمشیر (sword) + بار (a 'load'/'burden' used here in the idiom بار آورد) + آورد ('brought'/'bore'), literally 'he brought/shouldered the sword' = 'he armed/girded himself with the sword.' Object(s) precede the verb as is normal.
segments.s120-g78: This clause gives the result: سر = 'heads' (likely decapitated heads as a poetic motif). سرکشان in context names the people whose heads they are (an agent/possessive sense: 'of the rebels/insurgents'); the provided lemma glosses don't capture this sense, so its sense is marked -1. در کنار آورد is an idiom 'to bring/put (something) beside/at one's side' (در کنار = 'beside', آورد = 'brought').
segments.s120-g79: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. It governs the two coordinated past actions: پیغام بشنید ('he heard the message') and نامه بخواند ('he read the letter'); both verbs are simple past narrative forms used in sequence, joined by و.
segments.s120-g80: دژم گشت is 'he became sullen' (دژم = sullen/grim; گشت = became/turned). و اندر شگفتی بماند = 'and remained in astonishment': اندر = 'in/within', شگفتی = 'astonishment/wonder', and بماند ('he remained') is the verb indicating continued state. The sequence shows a past change of state followed by lingering astonishment.
segments.s120-g105: نپذرفت is the past third-person singular negative of پذیرفتن ('to accept'), literally 'did not accept' or 'refused'. ازو (from him) follows the verb to mark source; the list جامه و اسپ و زر are the direct objects linked by و ('and'). The verb-front order here reflects Persian poetic inversion (verb before objects) but the semantic structure is 'he refused to accept X from him'.
segments.s120-g106: که introduces the reason/cause here ('for' / 'because'). ننگ آمدش is an idiomatic expression: آمدش (آمد + enclitic ش = 'came to him') and ننگ ('shame') together mean 'shame befell him' or 'it would be shameful for him'. زان is the archaic form of از آن ('because of / from that') and points back to the offered items; کلاه و کمر are the nouns 'hat' and 'girdle/belt' joined by و.
segments.s120-g107: بیامد is past 'came' (aorist/simple past). دژم is a substantive/adjective meaning 'sullen' or 'gloom' and here functions as the thing that 'came'. از بر گاه اوی is a prepositional/locative phrase: از بر ('from before/of') + گاه (here used poetically as 'presence' or 'court/spot of being') + اوی (possessive/third-person enclitic = 'his'), so the phrase means 'upon/over his presence/court'.
segments.s120-g108: همه tیرе دید places the quantifier/adverb همه ('all') before the predicate تیره دید ('saw [them] dark/ominous'). دید is past 'saw'; اختر و ماه اوی is the object phrase 'the stars and the moon (of him / before him)', with اوی again marking possession/association. The line means he perceived all the celestial lights as dark—a poetic way to say his mood made the heavens look bleak.
segments.s120-g109: برون آمد is a compound of the directional adverb برون ('out') and the past verb آمد ('came'), meaning 'came out/stepped out'. از شهر مازندران is the origin phrase 'from the city of Mazandaran' with از marking source and شهر = 'city'. Word order places the directional adverb before the verb, which is common in classical/poetic Persian.
segments.s120-g110: سرش is 'his head' (سر + enclitic ش). گشته is the perfect/participial form of شدن meaning 'has become/turned', and بد is the predicate adjective 'bad/unfavorable' — together 'his head became ill/upset' (i.e., he was troubled). زان is the archaic 'از آن' ('from/ because of that') introducing the cause, followed by سخنها گران, 'the speeches/words (سخنها) being weighty/serious' — گران functioning as an adjective 'heavy/weighty'.
segments.s120-g111: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. آمد is the past 'came'. به نزدیک شاه is a prepositional phrase 'to/near the king' (به + نزدیک + شاه), and اندرون ('inner/within') specifies the inner chamber/inner court; the whole reads 'when he came near/into the king's inner presence'. The order places the temporal clause marker first as is common in classical Persian.
segments.s120-g112: دل کینه‌دارش is a noun phrase: دل ('heart') + کینه‌دارش (کینه‌دار = 'bearing a grudge/resentful' with enclitic ش = 'his'), so 'his grudge-bearing heart'. پر از is the fixed expression 'full of', and جوش خون ('josh-e khun') is an idiom: جوش here means 'boiling/surge' and خون 'blood', together 'a boiling/surge of blood'—a vivid metaphor for violent anger. The phrase portrays intense, blood-boiling hatred.
segments.s120-g97: چو begins a conditional/temporal clause ('if/when'). من is the 1st‑person subject. تنگ روی is a descriptive phrase ('tight‑faced', i.e. sullen or reserved); تنگ qualifies روی ('face'). اندر آرم is a classical 1st‑person verb form (آرم) meaning 'I withdraw / I stay inside' (آر- + -م); the whole chunk means 'if I go in / keep to myself'. بروی is the verb رفتن (بـ + روی) in an older/prescriptive form meaning 'go/leave' and functions here as the resulting motion/action in the clause chain (classical sequencing of verbal actions).
segments.s120-g98: سرآید is a 3rd‑person verb meaning 'comes / befalls' (used impersonally here). شما را is the indirect object marked with the accusative/topic marker را ('upon you'). همه modifies گفت‌وگوی that follows and means 'all / the whole of'; گفت‌وگوی is the noun 'conversation / talk'. Word order is verb → indirect object → modifier → noun, a common poetic order meaning 'all conversation will come upon you.'
segments.s120-g99: نگه کرد is the past simple compound 'he cast a look / he looked' (نگه + کرد). رستم is the subject; به marks the object/target of the look ('toward / at'). روشن‌روان is a proper name or epithet ('bright‑soul / light‑hearted') functioning as the first object of that gaze.
segments.s120-g100: This is a simple list of further objects introduced by repeated به/و: به شاه و سپاه ... به = 'toward/at'; شاه 'the king'; سپاه 'the army/host'. رد here is a noun listed between سپاه and پهلوان; in this context it functions as 'reply/answer/refutation' (a spoken response or counter) and thus belongs with the catalogue of persons/things examined. پهلوان 'champion/hero' closes the list. The repeated و simply connects the items in the enumerative sequence.
segments.s120-g101: نیامدش is a past negative compound meaning 'it did not come to him' or idiomatically 'he did not accept / it did not occur to him' (ش is the 3rd‑person enclitic object 'to him'). با marks the means ('by/with'); مغز here means 'core/essence' (i.e. the gist or force), and گفتار 'speech / words'. اوی is a possessive/3rd‑person marker here ('his'), so the phrase means 'his core words / the kernel of his speech' — altogether: 'his mind did not take in the force of his speech.'
segments.s120-g102: سرش is سر + the 3rd‑person enclitic ش 'his' = 'his head' (can mean head, spirit, or resolve). تیزتر شد is the comparative adjective تیز + comparative suffix -تر plus شد 'became' = 'became sharper/more eager'. به پیکار اوی is a prepositional phrase: به 'to/for' + پیکار 'battle/fight' + اوی 'him/with him' (3rd person); the whole means 'his resolve sharpened for battle with him.' Note the classical juxtaposition of possessive enclitics and adjectives: adjective+شد conveys a change of state.
segments.s120-g103: تهمتن is an epithet/name (Tahmātan/the mighty one) as subject. چو برخاست is a temporal clause: چو 'when' + برخاست 'he rose/rose up/departed' (past). کاید here is a classical word meaning 'design / (secret) purpose / plan' (used as a nominal comment on intention) or can indicate intent; in this line it signals purpose or resolve connected to 'به راه' ('on the road' / 'set out'). به راه is the prepositional phrase 'on the road / along the way.'
segments.s120-g104: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past form meaning 'he commanded/ordered' (بـ+فرمود used for a lordly command). تا introduces a purpose/result clause here and means 'so that / in order that'. خلعت 'robe (of honour)' is the object to be brought. آرند is the classical subjunctive/optative 3rd‑person plural of آوردن 'they bring' (here used in the content clause 'that they bring'). شاه 'king' is the beneficiary/goal (understood as 'bring the robe to the king' by classical word order).
segments.s120-g25: بدان here is a fused classical sequence meaning 'به آن' ('with/at that'); خنده is the noun 'laughter'. اندر introduces a local/frame sense 'in/at', and بیفشارد is a third‑person verbal form meaning 'pressed/squeezed' (literally 'he pressed'). چنگ is a concrete noun 'claw' (used metaphorically for a clenched hand). The word order is demonstrative/temporal > noun > locative > verb > object: 'with that laugh he pressed (his) claw inward.'
segments.s120-g26: ببردش is a verbal construction meaning 'it/that took (him/it)' or 'it carried it away' (here impersonal/resultive reading: 'it took away'); رگ is 'vein'. از + دست gives the source 'from the hand'. وز here is the fused sequence و + ز (and + از) introducing the next clause 'and from', while روی رنگ is a noun phrase 'face‑colour/complexion' (رنگ meaning complexion). Put together: 'the vein left his hand, and the color left his face.'
segments.s120-g27: بشد هوش is an idiomatic copular/passive-like sequence 'هوش شد' = 'awareness came/was roused' — بشد is the verbal 'became/it happened'. از آن مرد marks the experiencer/holder 'in/with that man', and رزم آزمای is a descriptive compound 'battle‑testing' (رزم + آزمای) qualifying مرد: 'that battle-tried man.' Word order: verb + noun (result) + experiencer + adjectival compound.
segments.s120-g28: ز بالای اسب = 'from the top of the horse' (ز = poetic 'from', بالای = 'top/above', اسب = 'horse'). اندر آمد = a fused verbal phrase 'came in/stepped in' here meaning 'came down' or 'dismounted', and به پای = 'to the foot/ground' (به = to/towards, پای = foot/ground). The clause thus describes movement: 'he came down from atop the horse to the ground.'
segments.s120-g29: یکی شد is an idiomatic way to say 'someone rose/one person went' (یکی = 'someone/a certain one', شد = 'happened/occurred/went' in narrative use). بر شاه = 'to the king' (بر here = toward/to), and مازندران names the realm. The whole segment means 'a certain one went to the king of Mazandaran.'
segments.s120-g30: بگفت = 'he told/said to him' (perfect/past reporting verb). آنچ دید = 'that which he saw' (آنچ = 'that which', دید = 'saw'). از کران تا کران is an idiomatic range 'from edge to edge' (از = from, کران = edge/end, تا = 'to/as far as'). Together: 'he told (the king) what he had seen from one end to the other.'
segments.s120-g31: سواری = 'a rider/horseman'. که نامش کلاهور بود is a relative clause 'whose name was Kalahur' (که = who/that, نامش = his name, کلاهور a proper name here, بود = was). The clause identifies the person: 'a rider whose name was Kalahur.'
segments.s120-g32: که at the start gives a causal/explanatory tone 'for/because'. مازندران زو is 'Mazandaran because of him' (ز = از 'from/of' in poetic order + و representing او 'him' fused as زو). پر از شور بود = 'was full of ardor' (پر از = full of, شور = passion/ardor, بود = was). The clause explains that Mazandaran was filled with excitement on his account.
segments.s120-g9: This is a prepositional/locative phrase placed before the verb: به (to/towards) + زیر (under/beneath) + اندرون (the inner part, ‘inside’). باره‌ای (from باره = load/burden) + گامزن (one who walks/strider) forms a noun phrase 'a load-bearing marcher/porter.' Word order is poetic: the place and the descriptor come up front to introduce who appears in the interior.
segments.s120-g10: The clause identifies the person introduced earlier: یکی ('a certain one') + ژنده (literally 'rag' / 'ragged' used adjectivally) + پیلست (an obscure/rare form) describing his garb; گویی functions here in the idiomatic sense 'as if' (poetic simile), and به تن = 'on the body' (idiomatically 'worn'). The verb is omitted (typical in poetry); the sequence describes appearance rather than performing an action.
segments.s120-g11: چو introduces a temporal clause ('when') and is followed by the past tense verb بشنید ('he heard'). سالار مازندران ('the commander/lord of Mazandaran') is the patient/subject of that verb; the verb appears before the full noun phrase because of poetic inversion.
segments.s120-g12: ز گردان ('from the troop') is a prepositional phrase marking the source; گزین کرد is the light-verb construction from گزینیدن/گزین کردن meaning 'to choose/selected' (گزین = choose + کرد = did). چندی سران ('several leaders') is the quantified object phrase; the verb گزین کرد governs that object.
segments.s120-g13: بفرمودشان is a past/honorific perfect-like form 'he commanded them' (فرمود + شان). تا here marks a result/temporal limit ('until' / 'so that'), and چپیره شدند is a predicate meaning 'they became/assumed the (state) chaper—' (چپیره is a rare/dialectal noun; the clause says they reached that condition). شدند is past plural 'they became'.
segments.s120-g14: This clause states the reception: هژبر is a proper name (the person involved); ژیان is an object marked by را (the definite object marker); پذیره شدند is a passive/compound verbal idea 'they received/accepted/took in' with شدند as the past plural auxiliary — 'they received Hežbar Zhiyān.'
segments.s120-g15: چو ('when') introduces a temporal clause. چشم تهمتن is a possessive noun phrase 'Tahmatan's eye/gaze' (چشم = gaze/look) and بدیشان ('upon them' / 'to them') is a prepositional phrase; رسید is the past-tense verb 'reached/fell on.' Together: 'when Tahmatan's gaze fell on them.'
segments.s120-g16: This gives what he saw: به ره ('on/along the way') + بر درختی ('on a tree') set the location; گشن شاخ is a noun phrase where گشن (literally 'hungry', here adjectival/qualifying) modifies شاخ ('branch; bough'). دید is the past verb 'he saw.' Word order places the locative phrases before the object and verb, as is common in narrative poetry.
segments.s120-g81: به رستم = a prepositional phrase 'to Rostam' (به + name) placed before the verb for emphasis. چنین گفت = 'said thus/so' (چنین adverb + گفت 'said'); کاین introduces the content clause (که + این contracted) 'that this...'. جست و جوی is the compound 'جست‌و‌جو' ('search, seeking') split into جست + و + جوی; here it is a noun phrase governed by the preceding کاین.
segments.s120-g82: چه باید = a rhetorical interrogative 'what/why should' (چه + باید). همی is a classical/progressive particle meaning 'still/continually' or emphatic ongoing action. خیره (used predicatively/adverbially) = 'staring/dumbstruck'. این گفت‌وگوی 'this conversation' is the noun phrase that follows and is what the subject is staring at; the subject ('you') is implied and the copula is omitted (typical in Persian).
segments.s120-g83: بگویش is the imperative of گفتن ('say/tell') with the enclitic ش = 'to him' (بگو + ی + ش). که introduces the reported content clause. سالار ایران تویی is a predicate clause with the copula تویی 'you are' at the end: 'you are the chief/commander of Iran.'
segments.s120-g84: اگرچه is a concessive conjunction 'although/even though.' دل و چنگ is a paired noun phrase 'heart and چنگ' (a poetic pairing; چنگ can mean 'harp' or 'claw/talon' — here linked with شیران 'lions' so it functions metaphorically as strength/talon). شیران is the genitive 'of the lions.' تویی again is the copula 'you are' final in the clause.
segments.s120-g85: منم is emphatic 'I am' (من + emphatic enclitic). شاه مازندران is a genitive phrase 'king of Mazandaran.' با سپاه is a following prepositional phrase 'with an army' that qualifies the speaker's state/position.
segments.s120-g86: Two parallel prepositional phrases introduced by بر 'on/upon': بر اورنگ زرین = 'upon a golden throne' (اورنگ 'throne', زرین 'golden'), and بر سر کلاه = 'on (the) head, a cap' (سر 'head' takes کلاه 'hat/cap'); the second بر repeats the preposition for the parallel structure.
segments.s120-g87: مرا = object 'me' (م + را here split in the data). بیهده خواندن is a verbal construction where خواندن is used in the sense 'to call/regard' (i.e. 'to call [someone] worthless'). پیش خویش = 'in your presence' / 'before oneself' (پیش = 'in the presence of'; خویش is reflexive 'self' — data is split into خوی + ش). The whole phrase is an infinitival/verbal-noun clause 'to call me worthless in your presence.'
segments.s120-g88: نه ... نه ... is the standard 'neither ... nor ...' construction. رسم کیان = 'the custom/practice of the Kayanids/kings.' بد here negated by the surrounding نه ... نه ... functions as 'not right/acceptable' (i.e. 'not (the) custom'). آیین پیش = 'a practice/ceremony in the presence (of others)' — together the clause denies that calling the speaker worthless is either a royal custom or an acceptable practice before others.

sentences.s120-s9: He pulled (it) off, and when he took the gambeson into his palm, the whole army stood amazed.
sentences.s120-s10: He threw it down; when he drew near them, many horsemen came beneath the boughs.
sentences.s120-s11: One seized his hand and pressed it, and they kept tormenting him with that trial.
sentences.s120-s12: Rostam of Piltan laughed at him; the eyes of that assembly became fixed on him in astonishment.
sentences.s120-s1: When the letter, sealed with a seal, was brought in to the king, Rostam, the world‑seeker, had traversed the road.
sentences.s120-s2: He cast a heavy mace onto the saddle when he came near Mazandaran.
sentences.s120-s3: The king was informed that when Kavus had sent the letter he had sent (men) in pursuit.
sentences.s120-s4: A messenger like the grim Hejbar — with a lasso on his cloak looped over his bent thumb.
sentences.s120-s33: He looked, set him down at his meal, and asked Kavus about his army.
sentences.s120-s34: He spoke of the long road and the hardships — how you rode through the downs and the ups.
sentences.s120-s35: After that Rostam said to him, 'You have strength in your arm and your flank.'
sentences.s120-s36: He answered thus: 'I am your servant, if I take on the service myself.'
sentences.s120-s25: He squeezed Kalahur’s claw hard — the nail fell down like a leaf from a tree.
sentences.s120-s26: Kalahur, his hand hanging, had flesh, skin, and nails ripped/collapsed.
sentences.s120-s27: He brought him in and displayed him and spoke with the king, saying that one cannot hide pain on oneself.
sentences.s120-s28: Reconciliation suits you better than war; do not make your heart narrow.
sentences.s120-s29: You have no match against such a champion; if he becomes tamed, there is none better than Saav.
sentences.s120-s30: Let us accept (it) from the city of Mazandaran, and let us bestow (it) upon the lesser and the greater.
sentences.s120-s31: Thus we will make such a difficult hardship easy, so that it may happen that we frighten the life (i.e. terrify him).
sentences.s120-s32: Tahmatan also came at that moment; bring (or: he brought) the lion of life to the king.
sentences.s120-s17: Like a living leopard of savage disposition, he had no habit except to desire the business of war.
sentences.s120-s18: He summoned a full reception and set the brave man upon the revolving wheel (of fate/sky).
sentences.s120-s19: He said to him, 'Be sent forward as a messenger; make your skills appear afresh.'
sentences.s120-s20: Do it so that Rakhsh becomes full of shame, and that warm (tears of) shame come into his eyes.
sentences.s120-s21: Kalahur came forward like a male lion before the sovereign — a brave man.
sentences.s120-s22: He questioned — questioning like a leopard; grim‑faced, thereupon he struck/gave him a claw.
sentences.s120-s23: He pressed (with) the claw; the lofty elephant, from the pain, had his hand turn the color of indigo.
sentences.s120-s24: He turned and kept his thoughts away from him; he had a man who bore an inscribed tablet from the sun.
sentences.s120-s45: Consider (this) and do not seek the thrones of the great, for from such preeminence humiliation will come upon you.
sentences.s120-s46: Turn your reins toward the seat/time of Iran, otherwise your appointed time will bring the spear upon you.
sentences.s120-s47: If I move my army from its place, you will not find your head (on your body).
sentences.s120-s48: You have fallen, without doubt, into confusion; choose one course and ready (or bend) your bow.
sentences.s120-s57: Whatever he saw and heard in Mazandaran, he made all manifest to the Shah of Iran.
sentences.s120-s58: After that he said to him, 'Think nothing of it; be brave and marshal a host to fight the divs.'
sentences.s120-s59: Consider the brave men and the ranks of that assembly as if they were contemptible in my sight.
sentences.s120-s37: Where he was, I would not meddle in the affair; he is a champion with a host and horsemen.
sentences.s120-s38: Then he gave him the famed letter — the message for the ambitious, self-willed seeker of the world.
sentences.s120-s39: He said that he had girded his sword; he had brought the rebels' heads to his side.
sentences.s120-s40: When he heard the message and read the letter, he became sullen and was left in astonishment.
sentences.s120-s53: He refused to accept from him robes, a steed, and gold, for that hat and girdle would bring him shame.
sentences.s120-s54: A sullen gloom came upon his presence; he saw all the stars and the moon as dark.
sentences.s120-s55: He came out of the city of Mazandaran; his head was troubled by those weighty words.
sentences.s120-s56: When he came near the king's inner presence, his heart—full of hatred—was filled with a boiling of blood.
sentences.s120-s49: If I put on a sullen face and keep myself within, all the talk will fall upon you.
sentences.s120-s50: Rostam cast his gaze on Rowshan‑ravan, and on the king and the army and the reply/answer and the champion.
sentences.s120-s51: His mind did not accept his speech; his head grew keener for fighting him.
sentences.s120-s52: When Tahmatan rose, (he) set out on the road; he commanded that they bring the robe to the king.
sentences.s120-s13: At that laugh he drew his claw inward; the vein left his hand and the color drained from his face.
sentences.s120-s14: Awareness came upon that battle‑tested man; he dismounted from the top of his horse to the ground.
sentences.s120-s15: Someone went to the king of Mazandaran and told him what he had seen from one edge to the other.
sentences.s120-s16: A rider whose name was Kalahur — for Mazandaran was filled with ardor because of him.
sentences.s120-s5: Into the inner quarters strode a load-bearer; a certain ragged fellow clad in elephant-hide, as if upon his body.
sentences.s120-s6: When the commander of Mazandaran heard, he selected several leaders from the troop.
sentences.s120-s7: He commanded them; until they were in position, they received Hežbar Zhiyān.
sentences.s120-s8: When Tahmatan's eye fell upon them, he saw on the road, upon a tree, a large/gnarled bough.
sentences.s120-s41: He said to Rostam thus: "What is this search for, and why do you stand there staring at this conversation?"
sentences.s120-s42: Tell him that you are the commander of Iran, even though you are the heart and the talon of lions.
sentences.s120-s43: I am the king of Mazandaran with an army, seated on a golden throne and with a cap on my head.
sentences.s120-s44: To call me worthless before you is neither the custom of the kings nor a proper practice in one's presence.
